question.text: TWRR (Time-Weighted Rate of Return) differs from IRR (MWRR) in that TWRR:

answer.text: Gives more weight to periods with larger investments
Eliminates the impact of cash flow timing, measuring pure investment performance independent of capital flows
Always produces a higher return than IRR
Is only used for listed equity portfolios

explanation.text: **The Logic:** TWRR measures investment performance independent of cash flow timing — it shows how well the manager invested, regardless of when capital was called or returned. IRR (MWRR) captures the investor's actual experience including cash flow timing effects. **The Trap:** TWRR is useful for evaluating manager skill, while IRR is useful for evaluating investor returns. A manager who makes great investments but has poor cash flow timing will show good TWRR but mediocre IRR. **Cross-Exam Context:** TWRR vs MWRR distinction is tested in XIX-A and XXI-B (Portfolio Managers). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Present both TWRR and IRR to sophisticated investors. TWRR shows the manager's investment skill; IRR shows the actual return after considering when capital was deployed. Large early drawdowns followed by quick exits inflate IRR relative to TWRR.
